items.title: Credit default risk on tabular loan data
items.scenario: A bank predicts whether a loan will default using income, debt ratio, and credit history. A single deep tree overfits rare default cases. Gradient boosting starts with the base default rate, then adds shallow trees that each tighten predictions for high-debt applicants, then for thin-file borrowers, until validation AUC stops improving.
items.explanation: Sequential residual fitting lets each tree patch a specific slice of errors left by prior trees—ideal for heterogeneous tabular patterns.

items.title: House price regression with mixed features
items.scenario: A real-estate model predicts price from square footage, zip code, and age. The first boosting round captures the broad sqft trend; later trees add corrections for old homes in premium zip codes where sqft alone under-prices.
items.explanation: With MSE loss, each tree fits price residuals, so the ensemble gradually bends the prediction surface without one monolithic tree.

items.title: Click-through rate with class imbalance
items.scenario: An ads team predicts clicks from user and ad features. Log-loss gradient boosting starts near the low base click rate, then successive trees increase scores for high-intent segments and decrease them for low-intent impressions.
items.explanation: Classification boosting chases log-loss gradients; shallow trees with shrinkage help avoid overfitting the tiny positive class.
